claims.cite: preparation of the sense-organ alone by concentration, collyrium and the like

claims.name: samskārayate
claims.cite: preparation of the object for the apprehension of its smell

rejected_readings.label: unified_samskara
rejected_readings.rendering: Both sense-organ and object must always be prepared together to enable perception.
rejected_readings.why: The verse distinguishes two independent pathways: organ-preparation (concentration improves the eye) operates 'while leaving the object untouched,' and object-preparation (crushing readies scent) operates 'while doing nothing to the nose.' Requiring both falsifies this independence.

rejected_readings.label: reverse_causation
rejected_readings.rendering: The hidden property (scent) itself causes the object to be prepared, rather than the object's preparation revealing the property.
rejected_readings.why: Reverses the direction of causation. The commentary shows that processing prepares/readies the object's gandha for apprehension; the gandha does not do the preparing. A sort error since gandha is a property (not a power) and cannot be a samskara-source.

notes: Verse 1.79 establishes two parallel pathways to manifestation via saṃskāra: (1) organ-preparation through concentration and collyrium (shown by the eye-salve example) and (2) object-preparation through processing (shown by crushing sandalwood). These are independent mechanisms with equal standing as empirical precedents that both apply to word/sound, positioning language within a general theory of manifestation rather than as a special case.
